name: Sword in Stone
description: The Sword in Stone is a legendary weapon and a central artifact in Arthurian mythology. It is most famously known as the sword that only the rightful king of Britain could draw from the stone or anvil in which it was embedded. The sword's origins vary depending on the source. In some versions, it is Excalibur, placed in the stone by Merlin to test the worthiness of potential kings after the death of Uther Pendragon. In other versions, it is a separate sword altogether, and Excalibur is later bestowed upon Arthur by the Lady of the Lake. The act of pulling the sword from the stone is not merely a feat of strength; it is a testament to the character, nobility, and destiny of the one who succeeds. It signifies divine right and marks Arthur as the chosen leader. The sword itself is often described as being exceptionally crafted, imbued with magical properties, and a symbol of justice and power. Its presence in the stone represents the fractured state of Britain, awaiting a true leader to unite and restore order. The Sword in the Stone is not just a weapon; it is a symbol of legitimacy, destiny, and the enduring hope for a righteous ruler. The act of drawing it solidifies Arthur's claim to the throne and sets him on his path to becoming the legendary King Arthur. Its removal also speaks to the themes of worthiness, fate, and the responsibility that comes with wielding power.
subtitle: The Sword of Destiny and Kingship
origin: Placed in stone by Merlin or other magical means.
currentLocation: Unknown, believed to be lost to time.

keyMyths.title: Arthur Draws the Sword
keyMyths.description: Arthur, initially as a squire, pulls the sword from the stone when no one else can, proving his worthiness to be king. This act establishes his claim to the throne and marks the beginning of his reign.

keyMyths.title: The Tournament
keyMyths.description: A tournament is held to find the one who can draw the sword, but many fail. Arthur, unaware of his destiny, inadvertently performs the feat, shocking everyone present.

sources.author: Sir Thomas Malory
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Arthurian legends.
